main_sections.title: The Tunnel
main_sections.prose_summary:
The Tunnel, written by Ruskin Bond, is a short story set in an Indian jungle. Suraj, a curious and adventurous boy, cycles out to a railway tunnel cut through a forested hill. He watches a steam engine roar through, and then walks the tunnel's dark, smoky interior just to experience it. On the hillside beyond, he glimpses a flash of orange and gold — a leopard. He meets the tunnel's sole watchman, Sunder Singh, an old man who feels a proprietary affection for the tunnel, the jungle, and even the leopard that periodically visits the area. Sunder Singh invites Suraj to return that evening to watch the night mail pass through.

Suraj comes back at dusk. The two sit together, drinking tea and listening to the forest's nighttime sounds — a barking deer, a fox, a nightjar, the creaking of trees. Sunder Singh makes his lamp-lit inspection of the tunnel. Inside, he hears something: a padded footfall, rustling leaves. He suspects the leopard is in the tunnel. With the night mail due in minutes, they cannot let it be killed. Suraj, despite his dry throat and fear, follows Sunder Singh into the dark. Their combined shouting drives the leopard out. The train thunders through, and the moment is exhilarating.

The story closes a week later: Suraj is on the night mail itself, accompanying his father to Delhi. In the darkness, he glimpses the distant lamp of Sunder Singh's hut and feels a quiet, warm connection to his friend — the lonely watchman who lights the dark for trains and leopards alike. The story's themes are curiosity, courage, unlikely friendship between generations, and a reverence for jungle life. Bond's prose captures the sensory richness of forest sounds, the power of a steam engine, and the intimacy of two people in darkness doing a brave, small thing together.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us do these activities before we read. What kind of an adventure activity would you like to be a part of? Choose the option(s) that best applies to you (questions 1–4 on personality type), and discuss with your classmates which label best applies to you: Bold Explorer, Imaginative Dreamer, Energetic Planner, or Thoughtful Creator.

question_bank.stem: How would you like to spend your weekends? (i) Trying new activities (ii) Reading a book (iii) Spending time with friends (iv) Learning something new
Which of these places would you prefer to explore? (i) Dense forests and clear streams (ii) Magical lands with fairies (iii) Busy cities with interesting people (iv) Quiet places to think and create
options: Quiet places to think and create

question_bank.stem: Which one of these activities would you prefer to do? (i) Climbing a mountain or exploring a jungle (ii) Finding a hidden treasure or discovering a secret world (iii) Solving puzzles or guessing riddles with friends (iv) Making something creative or building a model
options: Climbing a mountain or exploring a jungle
Finding a hidden treasure or discovering a secret world
Solving puzzles or guessing riddles with friends
Making something creative or building a model

question_bank.stem: What do you think about teamwork? (i) I love leading the team. (ii) I enjoy working with others. (iii) I prefer sharing ideas with friends. (iv) I like working alone.
options: I prefer sharing ideas with friends.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Section I — Let us discuss (after Part I of the story). Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: Suraj compares the train to a magical creature. Pick the phrases that convey the meaning.
Suraj knew that the train was close by because ____________________.
What did Suraj see inside the tunnel?
Select the correct option. What does Sunder Singh convey about the tunnel? He says that ____________________ (it is damp and sticky inside / he has lent it to the Government).
options: it is damp and sticky inside
he has lent it to the Government

question_bank.stem: The watchman has created a system through which he informs the locomotive driver about any danger in the tunnel. Refer to the text and complete the flow chart. (Day: Walks the length of the tunnel → (i) _____ → No danger found → (ii) _____ OR Danger found → (iii) _____. Night: (iv) _____ → (v) _____ → Waves his lamp to warn the locomotive driver.)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Section II — Let us discuss (after Part II of the story). Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: Sunder Singh knew that the leopard was in the tunnel because ____________.

Why do you think Suraj was unable to do any shouting in the tunnel in the beginning? (i) He was afraid of the leopard. (ii) He didn't want to disturb the leopard. (iii) He was trying to listen for the leopard. (iv) He didn't really believe Sunder Singh.
options: He was afraid of the leopard.
He didn't want to disturb the leopard.
He was trying to listen for the leopard.
He didn't really believe Sunder Singh.

question_bank.stem: What made the leopard disappear into the darkness?
How did Suraj know Sunder Singh was at the tunnel?
Why did Suraj's father take him along to Delhi?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — Section I. Read the following lines and answer the questions. (Extract 1: tunnel darkness passage; Extract 2: dialogue about the leopard in the tunnel.)

question_bank.stem: Write whether the following statement is true or false: Suraj looked back at the opening of the tunnel to check if the train was coming.

Suraj did not stop anywhere in the tunnel because _____________. A. there was nothing to see in the tunnel B. the tunnel was damp and sticky C. he was in a hurry to get out of the tunnel D. it was time for another train to come
options: there was nothing to see in the tunnel
the tunnel was damp and sticky
he was in a hurry to get out of the tunnel
it was time for another train to come

question_bank.stem: What does the sentence 'Till then, the cutting belonged to the jungle again' mean?
Suraj wanted to know the inside of the tunnel. What character trait of Suraj is highlighted here?
What was the watchman's concern? (from the leopard-in-tunnel extract)
Complete the sentence with a suitable reason. Sunder Singh was sure that the leopard in the tunnel would not attack him and Suraj because _____________.

Select the sentences that are true: A. Suraj wants to see the leopards in the jungle. B. Suraj is concerned about Sunder Singh's safety. C. The leopard in the tunnel attacks goats and stray dogs. D. Sunder Singh wants to protect Suraj. E. Sunder Singh knows all about the animals in the jungle.
Sunder Singh decides to take his axe with him. This tells us that he was _____________. A. violent B. cautious C. bold D. afraid

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — Section II. Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: What does Sunder Singh convey by saying that he has lent his tunnel to the Government?
According to Sunder Singh, 'It is safer in the forest than in the town'. What example does Sunder Singh give to support his opinion? What does this tell us about him?
Sunder Singh has become part of the jungle life. Support the view with a suitable reason.
Sunder Singh and Suraj form an unlikely friendship. What draws them together?
Why is Sunder Singh referred to as a 'firefly'?
Do you think Suraj is an adventure-loving boy? Give reasons.

listening.transcript_text: AUDIO GUIDE (Museum Treasure Hunt): Welcome to the City Museum. We start our tour at the entrance hall. Here you will find the information booth and a display devoted to the history of this museum. To the right of the Entrance Hall you will find the library. On the left is the museum shop. Go straight from the Entrance Hall till you reach the Art Gallery. The gallery is famous for its collection of rare paintings from various parts of India. There are three doors that lead out of the Art Gallery. The first door on the left leads to the Decorative Art Gallery, where you will find items like jewellery, carvings, and textiles from Medieval India. The next door is temporarily closed due to renovation work. The third door on the right leads to the Weapons Room, where you will find an extensive collection of Indian weapons including bows, swords, shields, and helmets. From the weapons room take a left turn. You will find the world-famous Musical Instruments Section, where you can find musical instruments from every country in the world. The second room to the left in this gallery will take you to the Auditorium. Hidden here is your treasure! All the best to all. May the best team win!

tasks_verbatim.task_text: A famous museum in your town is organising a treasure hunt in the museum premises. You and your friends decide to participate in it. Each team is given an audio guide (recorded information) and a map to the museum. Listen to the audio recording and as you listen, mark your map with the route to take to find the treasure. (Rooms: library, auditorium, weapon room, art gallery, musical instruments hall.)

Anju is going on a trekking trip with her family during the summer vacation. They have reached the railway station. Anju calls the tour manager for directions to the trekking site. Work in pairs and have this conversation. Student A will play the role of Anju and mark the route on the map given below. Student B will play the role of the tour manager. Looking at the map, give directions to reach the trekking site from the railway station. You may use direction phrases from the box (turn left, turn right, keep going straight till, take the next left, take the next right, take the roundabout, take the second left, start from, on the opposite side, right around the corner, in front of, next to).

Write a descriptive paragraph about your experience of a train journey. Describe the sights, sounds, and feelings you experienced during the journey. Include details about the people around you, the scenery outside, and the atmosphere inside the train to make your paragraph vivid and engaging. Use these guidelines: (i) Start with how the journey began: boarding the train, finding your seat, and initial impressions. (ii) Describe the sounds: the whistle, chatter of passengers, rhythmic clatter of wheels. (iii) Mention the sights: changing scenery outside, hustle and bustle inside, interesting moments. (iv) Reflect on your feelings: excited, relaxed, curious?
Complete the following sentences using the suitable phrases from the text given in the box below (drifting smoke, approaching engine, sawing sound, flickering lights).

question_bank.stem: We could hear the ______________________ of the train long before it came into view at the station.
The street was completely dark but the little boy walked to his house guided by the ______________________ from the houses.
The ______________________ from the carpenter's workshop grew louder every hour.
From the balcony, she watched the ______________________ from her neighbour's chimney float across the backyard.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: The text has many 'sound words'. Some of these words are given in Column 1 and their meanings in Column 2. Match the words with their meanings. You can use a dictionary. (creaking, chatter, hissing, rustle, puffing — matched with 5 meanings.)

question_bank.stem: Match the sound words (creaking, chatter, hissing, rustle, puffing) with their meanings.
question_bank.table:
Word|Meaning
creaking|making a long low sound when a door or wooden object moves or is moved
chatter|repeated series of short high sounds made by birds or monkeys
hissing|making a noise like a long 's' sound
rustle|a soft low, dry sound made by the movement of things such as leaves, paper, etc.
puffing|blowing with short, quick blasts, as the wind

question_bank.stem: Complete the sentences using the correct form of the sound words: (1) The steam engine reached the station __________________ black smoke into the sky. (2) Suddenly the snake raised its hood and made a __________________ noise. (3) When the wind blew, I heard the __________________ of the wooden roof. (4) He heard a __________________ of leaves behind him when he was walking in the woods. (5) I get disturbed by the continuous __________________ of the monkeys in the neighbourhood.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read how 'would' is used in the watchman's routine actions in the text. Notice that 'would' is used for habitual or routine actions in the past. 'Would' and 'used to' both describe repeated past actions, but 'used to' indicates an action that no longer happens.

question_bank.stem: Why is the word 'would' used in 'Every day, before the train came through, he would walk the length of the tunnel'? (i) to make a polite request (ii) to express a desire (iii) to talk about a habit in the past
options: to talk about a habit in the past

question_bank.stem: Complete the sentences using the verb in brackets with the correct form of 'used to' or 'would': When I was a child we used to live in a village near a forest. Back then, we (i) _____________ (grow) vegetables in our back garden. Every day we (ii) _____________ (get up) early and go for a walk. The weather in summer (iii) _____________ (be) really nice. There was a lake near the house and we (iv) _____________ (go) there every Sunday for a picnic. Winters (v) _____________ (be) rather cold. In the evenings, my father (vi) _____________ (tell) us incredible stories and we (vii) _____________ (listen) with admiration.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Use punctuation marks — brackets, semicolon, and colon — to punctuate the following paragraph about exploring Charminar, Hyderabad.

question_bank.stem: Use semicolons and colons to punctuate the paragraph: 'We explored the busy by-lanes of Charminar, the heart of Hyderabad it was an exciting day. We wanted to do a few things buy bangles, taste biryani, and purchase pearl earrings, which are special to this area...'

tasks_verbatim.task_text: The Nation's pride Atal Tunnel is 9.02 kilometres long. It runs under the Rohtang Pass and has been certified as the 'World's Longest Highway Tunnel above 10,000 Feet'. The history of Indian Railways spans over 165 years — first passenger train ran between Bori Bunder (Mumbai) and Thane on 16 April 1853. You may refer to the given link to know more about Indian Railways.

main_sections.title: Travel
main_sections.poem_text:
The railroad track is miles away,
    And the day is loud with voices speaking,
Yet there isn't a train goes by all day
    But I hear its whistle shrieking.

All night there isn't a train goes by,
    Though the night is still for sleep and dreaming,
But I see its cinders red on the sky,
    And hear its engine steaming.

My heart is warm with the friends I make,
    And better friends I'll not be knowing;
Yet there isn't a train I wouldn't take,
    No matter where it's going.
main_sections.poem_appreciation_summary: Edna St. Vincent Millay's 'Travel' is a lyric poem in three stanzas that captures the speaker's restless, irresistible longing for travel and movement. The railroad — distant yet ever-present in imagination — becomes a symbol of freedom, opportunity, and connection. The poem's central tension lies in the contrast between the noisy, crowded day and the silent, dreaming night, yet in both states the speaker is haunted by the train: its whistle shrieking by day, its cinders glowing red by night. The final stanza broadens the poem from longing to love — for friends made along the way — and resolves in a joyful, unconditional acceptance of wherever the journey leads. The dominant tone is warm, enthusiastic, and content. The ABAB rhyme scheme drives the poem's forward momentum, and personification ('whistle shrieking') and imagery ('cinders red on the sky', 'day is loud') are the chief literary devices.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us do these activities before reading the poem. (I) Complete the following word web on travel (Modes of Transport, Reasons for Travel, Adventure Activities, Feelings, Destinations, Challenges). (II) You are going on a trip to an adventure camp — discuss questions 1–5 with classmates. (III) Discuss in pairs: What might be the reasons that people enjoy train journeys?

question_bank.stem: Complete the word web on travel: Modes of Transport (bus,...), Reasons for Travel (work,...), Adventure Activities (exploring,...), Feelings (joy,...), Destinations (riverside,...), Challenges (bad weather,...).

You are going on a trip to an adventure camp: (1) How will you travel to the adventure camp? (2) What things will you take along? (3) Why would you like to go there? (4) What are the activities you would like to do with your friends? (5) How much time will it take to complete your adventure trip?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss — the poem. Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: The main ideas of each stanza of the poem are given below. Write the number of the stanza against the matching main idea. (1) At night, the speaker imagines the train and hears its engine in the quiet. (2) The speaker feels a strong desire to take any train, no matter where it goes. (3) The speaker hears the train whistle during the day, even though the train is far away.)
question_bank.table:
Main Idea|Stanza Number
At night, the speaker imagines the train and hears its engine in the quiet.|
The speaker feels a strong desire to take any train, no matter where it goes.|
The speaker hears the train whistle during the day, even though the train is far away.|

question_bank.stem: Read the poem and underline the rhyming words in each stanza. Write the rhyming words from each stanza. (Example — Stanza i: away-day. Stanza ii: ? Stanza iii: ?)
Identify the rhyme scheme of the poem from the options given below: (1) AABB (2) ABCA (3) ABAB (4) ABBC
Identify the word in the poem that personifies the whistle of the train.

Tone refers to the attitude the poet has about the poem. Which of these words can be used to describe the poet's tone in this poem? (serious, excited, curious, worried, thoughtful, content, enthusiastic, critical, joyful)
Select the words that tell us what the train symbolises for the poet: (1) education (2) adventure (3) comfort (4) opportunities

The poet repeats the line '...there isn't a train goes by,' in all three stanzas to _______. (highlight the speaker's fascination with the train / suggest that the area has many trains passing through)
options: highlight the speaker's fascination with the train
suggest that the area has many trains passing through

question_bank.stem: The poem contrasts the busy day with the stillness of the night. What is the common factor in these?

Imagery is a literary device that uses descriptive language to create mental pictures and appeal to the reader's senses. Select the phrases from the poem that are examples of imagery. (One example given: 'day is loud'.)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — Section I. Read the extract ('My heart is warm with the friends I make, / And better friends I'll not be knowing...') and answer the questions. Section II — Answer questions on the full poem.

question_bank.stem: The line 'My heart is warm' refers to a heart that is ___. A. filled with worries B. full of love C. feeling upset D. experiencing anger
What does the speaker say about her current friends?
What does the line 'better friends I'll not be knowing' suggest about the speaker's feelings toward their current friends?
Complete the following suitably. The tone of the speaker in these lines is ______. (grateful / content)
What does the phrase 'cinders red on the sky' tell us about the train at night?
What is the significance of the repeated references to the train's whistle?
What does the line 'no matter where it's going' reveal about the speaker's attitude towards travel?
Do you think the poet actually sees and hears the train? Why or why not?

listening.transcript_text: ANNOUNCEMENT (School Trip to National Science Centre): Good morning students! I have some very exciting news to share with you all. The much-awaited educational trip to the National Science Centre will be held on 16 September, that is in two weeks! As you know this would be a fantastic opportunity to explore and learn more about the world around us. We will be starting from school at 8.00 in the morning and return by 5.30 in the evening. All of you must be in proper school uniform. Don't forget to carry your identity cards. Light refreshments will be provided at the museum. But do carry a water bottle and a packed lunch. You are not permitted to wear any jewellery or carry mobile phones. You may carry a notebook and a pen to make notes. Interested students must bring a permission slip signed by your parent and submit them to their class teachers by 10 September. It is going to be a fun trip so don't miss out. If you have any more questions, please contact Mr. Mukherjee, the excursion in-charge.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Listen to this announcement made in the morning assembly about a school trip to the National Science Centre. As you listen, circle the information that is not correct from statements 1–7.

question_bank.stem: Listen and identify which of these statements are NOT correct: (1) The trip to the National Science Centre is organised on 15 September. (2) Students will start from school at 8.00 in the morning and return by 5.00 in the evening. (3) Refreshment will not be provided during the trip. (4) Students are permitted to carry a phone, a notebook, and a pen. (5) The permission slip is to be signed by the class teacher. (6) The permission slip is to be submitted to your class teacher by 10 September. (7) Mr. Mukherjee is the Adventure Club in-charge.
Now, listen to the announcement again and rectify the sentences (write the correct version of each incorrect statement).

tasks_verbatim.task_text: When speaking in English, small differences in pronunciation can change the overall meaning of words. The letter 'c' has two sounds — /k/ (hard c, before a, o, u or consonant) and /s/ (soft c, before e, i, y). Work in pairs: pronounce the given words aloud keeping the rules of soft and hard 'c' pronunciation in mind. (Words: cell, cable, race, cake, decide, receive, concert, circle, bicycle, echo, uncle.)

question_bank.stem: Pronounce the following words aloud and classify them as hard 'c' (/k/) or soft 'c' (/s/): cell, cable, race, cake, decide, receive, concert, circle, bicycle, echo, uncle.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: A diamante poem is a poem shaped like a diamond that uses specific types of words like adjectives and –ing words. Read the example (Day / Bright, sunny / Dancing, playing, running / Out comes the sun, down goes the sun / Resting, listening, dreaming / Quiet, peaceful / Night). Now write a diamante poem that connects 'reading' and 'adventure' using the provided template. Share your poem with your classmates and the teacher.

We come across many interesting characters while travelling. Describe one such character that you met. Follow these steps: (1) Choose the character. (2) Create a first draft of your description including: (i) What is the name of the character? (ii) What does the character look like? (iii) What does the character do? (iv) What kind of person is she or he? (v) Include any line or incident from the text that you like the most. (3) Check your description for any spelling mistakes or errors. (4) Show your classmates your work and ask for suggestions. (5) Rewrite your description including all the changes and suggestions for improvement.

Read the expressions from the poem given in Column 1 and match them with what they indicate in Column 2 (miles away, cinders red, heart is warm, day is loud, engine steaming). Then use the Column 1 expressions to fill in the following blanks correctly.

question_bank.stem: Match the poetic expressions with their meanings: (1) miles away — (i) the speaker's affection for friends / (ii) fast, powerful movement / (iii) busy, noisy atmosphere / (iv) glow of sparks lighting the night sky / (v) distance of train from speaker; 2) cinders red; 3) heart is warm; 4) day is loud; 5) engine steaming.
question_bank.table:
Expression|Meaning
miles away|The distance of the train from the speaker
cinders red|The glow of the train's sparks lighting up the night sky
heart is warm|The speaker's affection for their friends
day is loud|The busy and noisy atmosphere during the day
engine steaming|The fast and powerful movement of the train

question_bank.stem: Use the expressions (miles away, heart is warm, engine steaming, cinders red, day is loud) to fill in the blanks: (1) After meeting her old friend, her ______ with the joy of rekindled memories. (2) As I arrived at the railway station I could hear the ______. (3) The marketplace is bustling, and the ______ with the chatter of shoppers and vendors. (4) The bonfire glowed brightly, throwing ______ into the dark night sky. (5) The sound of a train could still be heard _____ as the evening grew quiet.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: The poet uses 'shrieking' to tell us about the sound of a train's whistle. There are other words that tell us about different types of sounds. Match meanings in Column 1 with words in Column 2 (wailing, hollering, screeching, yelling, whimpering, bawling, screaming). Then complete the paragraph using suitable words from Column 2.

question_bank.stem: Match each meaning with the correct sound word: (1) shouting loudly, often in anger or excitement → (i) wailing / (ii) hollering; (2) crying out in fear or excitement → (iii) screeching; (3) crying loudly with grief or sorrow → (iv) yelling; (4) making a high-pitched, piercing sound → (v) whimpering; (5) calling out loudly, often in a casual or informal way → (vi) bawling; (6) crying softly in a low, broken voice → (vii) screaming; (7) crying loudly and uncontrollably.
question_bank.table:
Meaning|Word
shouting loudly, often in anger or excitement|yelling
crying out in fear or excitement|screaming
crying loudly with grief or sorrow|wailing
making a high-pitched, piercing sound|screeching
calling out loudly, often in a casual or informal way|hollering
crying softly in a low, broken voice|whimpering
crying loudly and uncontrollably|bawling

question_bank.stem: Complete the paragraph using suitable sound words: 'The street was filled with a variety of sounds. A shopkeeper was 1. ______ to attract customers, while a child nearby was 2. _______ at his friend to wait for him. From the other side of the road, a toddler was 3. ________ uncontrollably after dropping his ice cream, and his mother tried to calm him as he began 4. _________  softly. In the background, someone's loud 5. _________  voice added to the bustling noise of the busy evening.'

tasks_verbatim.task_text: The Mountain Railways of India (Darjeeling Himalayan Railway, Nilgiri Mountain Railway, and Kalka–Shimla Railway) are UNESCO World Heritage Sites. The Matheran Hill Railway and Kangra Valley Railway are on the tentative list. In groups of four, find out more about these heritage trains and make a presentation in the class.

question_bank.stem: In groups of four, find out more about the Mountain Railways of India (Darjeeling Himalayan Railway, Nilgiri Mountain Railway, Kalka–Shimla Railway, Matheran Hill Railway, Kangra Valley Railway) and make a presentation in class.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: The Indian Railways launched India's first indigenous semi-high-speed train, Vande Bharat Express, on 15 February 2019, on the New Delhi–Kanpur–Allahabad–Varanasi route running at 160 km/h. India's first underwater metro route in Kolkata started in March 2024 under the Hooghly River. Some of the underwater rail tunnels globally are the Thames Tunnel, Seikan Tunnel, and Channel Tunnel. In groups of four, find out more about these and make a presentation in class. Discuss which of these trains would be adventurous to travel in.

question_bank.stem: Find out more about the Vande Bharat Express, Kolkata underwater metro, and major global underwater rail tunnels. In groups of four, make a presentation in class. Discuss which of these trains would be the most adventurous to travel in.

main_sections.title: Conquering the Summit
main_sections.prose_summary: Conquering the Summit is an informational biography of Arunima Sinha, the first Indian female amputee to climb Mount Everest. In April 2011, Arunima — then 24, a national-level volleyball player from Ambedkar Nagar, Uttar Pradesh — was pushed from a moving train by thieves and lost her leg. Despite the devastating trauma, she made a vow while in hospital: to climb Everest with a prosthetic leg. She completed a basic mountaineering course at the Nehru Institute of Mountaineering in Uttarkashi, underwent eighteen months of rigorous training, and sought mentorship from Bachendri Pal, the first Indian woman to summit Everest. On 21 May 2013, after 52 exhausting days, Arunima reached the summit, displaying the Indian flag and leaving behind a photograph of Swami Vivekananda. She was awarded the Padma Shri (2015), the Tenzing Norgay National Adventure Award, and National Award for Best Female Mountaineer. By 2019 she had completed all Seven Summits, becoming the world's first female amputee to climb Mount Vinson in Antarctica. Her autobiography Born Again on the Mountain records this journey. Her central message — 'Never give up and work hard' — and Bachendri Pal's words ('you have conquered the Everest within you') frame the piece's themes of resilience, mental fortitude, and the power of a positive mindset.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us do these activities before we read. (I) Identify the activities represented by the icons given below. Share your answers with your classmates and the teacher. (II) Work in pairs and discuss: (1) Why do you think people undertake these activities? (2) How would they prepare for these activities? (3) Which one do you find the most exciting? Why? (III) Work in pairs to complete the word web: Qualities of a mountaineer.

question_bank.stem: Work in pairs and discuss: (1) Why do you think people undertake adventure sports/mountaineering? (2) How would they prepare for these activities? (3) Which one of them do you find the most exciting? Why?
Complete the word web: Qualities of a mountaineer.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss. Read the statements given below and explain each statement in your own words.

question_bank.stem: Explain in your own words: 'Arunima Sinha is considered a beacon of hope and resilience.'
Explain in your own words: 'Arunima decided to climb Mount Everest despite losing her leg.'
Explain in your own words: 'Bachendri Pal's guidance was crucial in Arunima's journey to the summit of Mount Everest.'
Explain in your own words: 'Arunima's achievements go beyond just physical endurance.'
Explain in your own words: 'Arunima's autobiography inspires readers around the world.'

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — Section I. Read the extract (Bachendri Pal's words to Arunima + Arunima's summit day) and answer the questions. Section II — Answer the following questions on the full text.

question_bank.stem: Bachendri Pal said that Arunima had conquered the Everest within her because ___________________.
How might Arunima have felt after what Bachendri Pal told her?
Choose a phrase from the extract which shows that reaching the summit of Mount Everest was tiring.

After her success when Arunima felt 'an overwhelming sense of achievement', she would have most likely said, "___________________.". (i) I am confident of my abilities (ii) I will accomplish this soon (iii) I am excited to begin this climb (iv) I finally realised my dream
options: I am confident of my abilities
I am excited to begin this climb

question_bank.stem: What did Arunima do in order to fulfill her ambition of conquering Mount Everest?
Arunima is a beacon of hope and resilience. Support this statement with evidence from the text.
Arunima's autobiography is titled Born Again on the Mountain: A Story of Losing Everything and Finding It Back. Why do you think this title is appropriate?
Arunima says, 'Failure is when we don't have goals worthy enough.' What would be the most likely impact of this statement on readers?

listening.transcript_text:
Speaker (i): As a parent, I fear the risks involved, especially with activities like rock climbing or paragliding. I'm quite worried about the safety risks of adventure sports. It's important to consider these risks before letting children participate.
Speaker (ii): Adventure sports are thrilling! All my friends agree; it is amazing. It's not just about fun; it's about pushing your limits and experiencing something new. I think it's worth trying, of course, with a trainer.
Speaker (iii): As organisers of adventure camps, we take safety very seriously. Our team ensures all equipment is regularly checked and maintained. Participants are given thorough training and are always supervised by professionals. We focus on minimising risks while still providing an exciting experience.
Speaker (iv): At our school, we organise adventure camps with activities like trekking and zip-lining. However, we stick to basic sports that are safe and manageable for students. We always prioritise safety, ensuring that all necessary precautions and measures are in place. Being careful is what we promote.
Speaker (v): In my opinion, sports should be about fitness and enjoyment, like track and field or swimming. Why risk your life for a fleeting moment of thrill just for a feeling of adventure? Traditional sports are safer and just as fulfilling!

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to five speakers share their opinion about adventure sports. As you listen match the statements 1–8 with the speakers (i)–(v). There are three extra statements that you do not need.

question_bank.stem: Match statements 1–8 with speakers (i)–(v). There are three extra statements. Statements: (1) Talks about having basic activities in adventure sports. (2) Finds adventure sports thrilling and exciting. (3) Praises the availability of a variety of activities. (4) Expresses concern about the risks in adventure sports. (5) Promotes trekking as a casual sport. (6) Invites applications from first-timers. (7) Focuses on the availability of training and supervision. (8) Finds exposure to risks unnecessary.
question_bank.table:
Statement|Speaker
Talks about having basic activities in adventure sports|iv
Finds adventure sports thrilling and exciting|ii
Expresses concern about the risks in adventure sports|i
Focuses on the availability of training and supervision|iii
Finds exposure to risks unnecessary|v

tasks_verbatim.task_text: In groups of four, select a role from 1–4 and speak about the views allotted to each using the prompts and vocabulary given. Take turns to express your opinions, agreement, and disagreement. Roles: (1) School Student — why Physical Education should be included in school activities; (2) Parent — concerns or support for child participating in PE; (3) Physical Education Teacher — share experiences and value of PE activities; (4) Sports Camp Organiser — explain how you ensure safety and why sports are beneficial.

(1) Read the letter given below and write the matching components. (2) Write a letter to the Principal of your school requesting her to organise an excursion to a nearby hill station for Grade 7 students of your school.

question_bank.stem: Read the sample formal letter (Request to join Adventure Club from Sreelakshmi, Grade 7-C) and identify and label each component (sender's address, date, receiver's address, subject, salutation, introduction, statement of request, expression of interest, complimentary close, signature line).
Write a formal letter to the Principal of your school requesting her to organise an excursion to a nearby hill station for Grade 7 students of your school.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the underlined phrases in sentences (i)–(v) and match them with their appropriate meanings from the box (genuine, sincere efforts / strength of mind / periods of time filled with tiring and demanding activities / an incident or experience that significantly changes a person's life / a source of inspiration or encouragement).

question_bank.stem: Match the underlined phrases with their meanings: (i) 'several exhausting days of practice' (ii) 'heartfelt attempts' (iii) 'life-altering event' (iv) 'mental fortitude' (v) 'beacon of hope'.
question_bank.table:
Phrase|Meaning
exhausting days|periods of time filled with tiring and demanding activities
heartfelt attempts|genuine, sincere efforts
life-altering event|an incident or experience that significantly changes a person's life
mental fortitude|strength of mind
beacon of hope|a source of inspiration or encouragement

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Complete the following table by filling in the gaps with correct parts of speech. (Nouns, Adjectives, Verbs (present), Adverbs — for: determination, relief, inspiring/inspire, resistance, success.)

question_bank.stem:
Complete the word-form table:
| Noun | Adjective | Verb (present) | Adverb |
| determination | determined | determine | determinedly |
| relief | ___ | ___ | relievedly |
| ___ | ___ | inspire | ___ |
| resistance | ___ | ___ | resistantly |
| success | successful | ___ | ___ |

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Study the highlighted articles (a, an, the) in the sentences from the text. 'A' and 'an' are indefinite articles; 'the' is the definite article. The choice between 'a' and 'an' depends on the initial sound of the word that follows.

question_bank.stem: Fill in the blanks with 'a', 'an', 'the', or 'x' (where no article is required): 'We planned (i) _______ adventurous trip to (ii) _______ remote island. (iii) _______ island was known for its beautiful landscapes and (iv) _______ diverse wildlife. On (v) _______ first day, we set out to explore (vi) _______ island's dense jungle. (vii) _______ guide led us through (viii) _______ thick foliage and showed us (ix) _______ hidden waterfall. In (x) _______ evening, we gathered around (xi) _______ campfire to share stories and enjoy (xii) _______ meal we cooked. (xiii) _______ experience was truly unforgettable, and we felt (xiv) _______ connection with (xv) _______ nature.'

Correct the article error in each sentence: (i) We waited for a hour before the bus finally arrived. (ii) The guide insisted on an uniform jacket for the group's safety during the trek. (iii) Mount K2 is a second highest peak in the world. (iv) A Ganga is a sacred river in India, known for its cultural importance. (v) Challenges are faced with courage and determination by a brave. (vi) A North Star has guided travellers for centuries.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Find out more information about the following adventure sports in India and complete the table given below (Name, Area, Preferred Time of the Year, Equipment Required): snow skiing, trekking, camping, river rafting, scuba diving.

question_bank.stem: Find out more information about these adventure sports in India and complete the table (Name, Area, Preferred Time of the Year, Equipment Required): snow skiing (snow-covered hills; skis, ski suit), trekking (Spring, Autumn, Winter; pole), camping (grassy areas; tent, torch), river rafting (all year round), scuba diving (scuba suit, mask, fins).

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Prepare an information booklet with fact sheets of five Indian mountaineers. Include the following information in each fact sheet: (1) name and photograph if available; (2) place of birth; (3) profession; (4) accomplishments — awards or honours; (5) training received; (6) sport known for (if any).

question_bank.stem: Prepare an information booklet with fact sheets of five Indian mountaineers, covering: name and photograph, place of birth, profession, accomplishments/awards, training received, and sport known for.
